1: Third year of Cyrus = 536 BCE. Daniel has served at court since 605 BCE — approximately 70 years of public service. He would be in his 80s by this point. The message is described as "true" (emet) and concerning a "great conflict (tzava gadol)" — the phrase implies extended warfare or tribulation ahead. The explicit authentication ("the vision was true and it concerned a great conflict") frames what follows as a reliable disclosure of what is about to unfold in world history.
2: The three-week mourning fast (21 days) frames the vision temporally. The first month of the year in the Jewish calendar is Nisan (March-April); a three-week fast beginning at the new month would overlap entirely with Passover (Nisan 14-21). The fact that Daniel maintained his mourning through Passover — the great feast of redemption — intensifies the gravity of his concern for his people. He was likely distressed about the state of the return from exile and the obstacles to rebuilding the temple (cf. Ezra 1-3).
3: The partial nature of the fast — "no choice food, no meat or wine, no lotions" — resembles mourning practice rather than the full fast of Yom Kippur (no food or water). The prohibition on oil/lotion was standard in mourning contexts (2 Samuel 12:20 — David resumed anointing himself after his son's death; Ruth 3:3 — Ruth anoints herself as a sign of restored relationship). This extended partial fast signals protracted intercessory grief, not a brief liturgical act.
4: The Tigris (Hiddekel) — one of the four rivers of Eden (Genesis 2:14), now the great river of the Assyrian/Babylonian heartland. Daniel's position on its bank echoes Ezekiel's visionary reception by the Chebar canal (Ezekiel 1:1, 3). The use of the ancient Eden-associated name links the revelation site to primordial creation geography — the visions Daniel receives concern ultimate reality.
5: The man clothed in linen (levush badim) — linen was the material of priestly garments (Leviticus 16:4; Ezekiel 9:2). The figure's clothing marks him as belonging to the heavenly court. Fine gold from Uphaz — Uphaz is of uncertain location; some identify it with Ophir (Job 28:16; 1 Kings 9:28), the source of the finest gold, suggesting superlative quality rather than a specific geography. The gold belt signals royal authority combined with priestly purity.
6: The theophanic description — body like chrysolite (tarshish), face like lightning (baraq), eyes like flaming torches, arms and feet like burnished bronze (nachoshet qalal), voice like a multitude — is the most elaborate angelic/divine description in Daniel, closely paralleling Ezekiel's kavod vision (Ezekiel 1:26-28) and John's vision of the risen Christ (Revelation 1:12-16). Whether this figure is angelic (Gabriel) or a divine appearance is debated; the similarity to Ezekiel's divine throne vision and Rev 1's Christ-figure suggests the description draws on the same heavenly archetype.
7: The pattern of Daniel alone seeing the vision while companions are overwhelmed with unexplained terror replicates the Sinai theophany pattern (Exod 19-20, where only Moses ascends; the people are terrified and flee) and Paul's Damascus road encounter (Acts 9:7, where companions heard but did not see). The terror experienced by those who do not see is more than psychological reaction — it is a response to genuine supernatural presence.
8: Daniel's physical response — strength failing, face going deadly pale — is the standard theophanic collapse pattern: Isaiah (Isaiah 6:5, "I am undone"), Ezekiel (Ezekiel 1:28, falling face down), John at Patmos (Revelation 1:17, falling as though dead). The approach of unmediated divine glory overwhelms human constitution; Daniel's physical extremity validates the reality and intensity of what he is experiencing.
9: Deep sleep (tardemah) — the same word used for Adam's sleep before Eve's creation (Genesis 2:21) and Abraham's sleep during the covenant-cutting ceremony (Genesis 15:12). In biblical usage, tardemah marks a threshold between ordinary consciousness and divinely induced receptivity — the sleeper is not self-willed but passive before the divine act. Face to the ground is the prostration posture before divine majesty.
10: The touch (naga') that sets Daniel "trembling on his hands and knees" is an act of commission — the same type of enabling touch appears in Isaiah's coal-to-lips (Isaiah 6:7) and Jeremiah's hand to mouth (Jeremiah 1:9). The movement from complete prostration to hands-and-knees is a staged restoration: first partial rising, then standing (Daniel 10:11), then receiving strength to stand fully (Daniel 10:19).
11: "Highly esteemed" (ish chamudot) — see note on Daniel 9:23. The commission formula "consider carefully (hitsoneyn) the words I speak to you, and stand upright (amad)" echoes the prophetic commissioning of Ezekiel (Ezekiel 2:1-2, "stand on your feet"). The act of receiving the divine word requires a posture of attentive standing — not prostration, not ease, but alert readiness.
12: "Since the first day you set your heart" — the divine response was immediate from Day 1 of Daniel's prayer. The 21-day delay was not because prayer was unheard but because of spiritual conflict. This is one of the most explicit biblical passages about the relationship between intercessory prayer and angelic warfare: prayer activates divine response; the delay in visible answer does not indicate unanswered prayer but ongoing battle in the heavenly realm.
13: Prince of the Persian kingdom — the concept of national celestial guardians (patron angels of nations) is rooted in Deuteronomy 32:8 LXX / Dead Sea Scrolls fragment (4QDeutj): "when the Most High gave the nations their inheritance, he separated the sons of man; he set the boundaries of the peoples according to the number of the sons of God." The MT's "sons of Israel" is a later harmonizing variant; the original text indicates that nations have heavenly patron-beings over them. Daniel 10 dramatizes this theology: the political opposition of Persia to Jewish restoration has a heavenly correlate in the "prince of Persia" resisting the angelic messenger. Michael, described as "one of the chief princes," is Israel's patron angel (Daniel 12:1; Revelation 12:7).
14: "What will happen to your people in the latter days (acharit ha-yamim)" — the same eschatological formula from Deuteronomy 4:30 (in your distress in the latter days); Numbers 24:14 (Balaam's final oracle); and consistently in the prophets. The phrase spans the range from near historical fulfillment to ultimate eschatological consummation; Daniel's visions operate simultaneously on both levels.
15: The messenger's words about cosmic conflict and the future of Daniel's people leave Daniel silenced — "I looked down at the ground and could not speak." The weight of what is disclosed overwhelms the capacity for response. This muteness before revelation is a recurring motif: Zechariah (Luke 1:20), Isaiah before the seraphim (Isaiah 6:5). The silence is not irreverence but the appropriate human response to unbearable gravity.
16: "One who appeared to be a man" (possibly a second angelic figure, or the same messenger) touches Daniel's lips — the lips being the organ of speech, the touch restores the capacity for response. The lip-touch parallels the coal to Isaiah's lips (Isaiah 6:7) and YHWH's hand to Jeremiah's mouth (Jeremiah 1:9) — each a commissioning act that enables the prophet to speak what he could not otherwise utter.
17: "How can I, your servant, speak with you, my lord? My strength is gone and I can hardly breathe" — the physical language of spiritual depletion: neshamah (breath) barely remaining. In Hebrew anthropology, breath is life (Genesis 2:7); its near-absence signals near-death. The double confession of weakness (koach) — first exhaustion, then breathlessness — is the human side of the theophanic encounter, expressing what direct proximity to the divine costs.
18: The messenger touches Daniel a second time, giving strength (chizzeqani, from H2388 chazaq, to strengthen) — the same root as Hezekiah's name (YHWH strengthens). The repeated acts of touch, strengthening, and commission build to the full restoration that enables Daniel to receive the revelation of Daniel 11-12. Divine commissioning always includes the provision of what is required to fulfill the commission.
19: "O man highly esteemed (ish chamudot), do not be afraid. Peace (shalom). Be strong, be strong." — The triple greeting form — address by title, peace-declaration, double imperative of strength — is the most elaborate angelic commission formula in Daniel. "When he spoke to me, I was strengthened" — the word itself carries the strength it commands, a feature of the divine word throughout the OT (Ps 29; Isaiah 55:10-11).
20: "Fight against the prince of Persia" — the angelic messenger will return to the cosmic conflict after delivering the revelation. Then the "prince of Greece (sar Yavan)" will come — anticipating the shift from Persian to Greek world empire that Daniel 11 will trace in detail. The succession of heavenly "princes" maps directly onto the political succession of world empires: Persia → Greece → Rome. What follows in Daniel 11 is the earthly narrative of what these heavenly battles determine.
21: Book of Truth (sefer emet) — the heavenly record of what will unfold, a document written before the events occur. This concept of the heavenly book appears also in Psalms 139:16 (all Daniel's days were written in the book before one came to be), Exodus 32:32-33 (the book of the living), and Revelation 5:1 (the sealed scroll). Michael, your prince — Israel has its own celestial patron, and he fights alongside the angelic messenger. The disclosure of this cosmic framework is itself protective: Daniel knows that despite earthly appearances, Israel is not abandoned in the heavenly court.
